---
title: "Chapter 7: Drill planning under uncertainty"
subtitle: "Sequential drill planning on the BC Golden Triangle regional prior"
sidebar: drill_planning
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## Setup

The earlier chapters build prospectivity maps. A prospectivity map is
a per-cell probability surface, and it doesn't tell you where to drill
*next* given what you've already drilled. Real exploration campaigns
are sequential: each hole costs money, returns information, and that
information should update the next-hole decision.

This chapter ports the methodology of *Intelligent Prospector* (Mern
et al., *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16:289, 2023; arXiv 2410.10610, 2024)
onto the BC Golden Triangle regional prior from Chapter 2.

## The result on synthetic terrain

![Four policies on a synthetic 30x30 stress test matching the 2024 Mern paper's conditions (correlated prior, noisy Gaussian sensor, 9 holes per episode, 17 episodes). Left: fraction of episodes that have found at least one true deposit at each drill step. POMCP finishes at 94%, BayesianGreedy at 82%, GreedyMean (the simplest baseline) saturates at 53% by hole four. Right: mean discovery rate per trajectory.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_b1_baseline_benchmark.png)

POMCP, the planner that updates its beliefs after every observation
and plans several steps ahead, finds at least one deposit in 94% of
episodes by the last hole. The simplest baseline (GreedyMean), which
just drills the highest-probability cell from the static prior,
saturates at 53% by hole four and stops improving. A middle baseline
(BayesianGreedy) that updates beliefs but doesn't plan ahead reaches
82%.

Two things are happening here. First, the prior carries spatial
correlation: a drill at cell A tells you something about cells B,
C, D within roughly a kilometer. The static-prior baseline ignores
that and leaves information on the table after every drill. Second,
POMCP simulates many possible futures before each pick and chooses
the action with the best expected total reward across those futures,
not just the cell that looks best right now. The first effect is the
big jump (GreedyMean to BayesianGreedy, 53% to 82%). The second is
the smaller tail win (BayesianGreedy to POMCP, 82% to 94%).

## Real-data check: BCGT retrospective

The chart above is a stress test on synthetic terrain. The real-data
check is the chart below, run on a 50x50 BCGT cell working area
(~25 km x 25 km, centered on the KSM cluster). 14 post-2010 BCGS
drill holes inside that area hit the 0.2% Cox-Singer Cu cutoff;
those 14 cells are the held-out targets. The 4 pre-2010 drilled
cells are excluded from the candidate set. Each policy is scored on
what fraction of the 14 post-2010 Cu+ cells it would have
recommended in its top-k% picks given a 625-cell trajectory.

![Three prior variants of the B.2 retrospective check. Left: smoothed MINFILE mineral-occurrence prior (informative but likely temporally contaminated since MINFILE is a current snapshot). Center: pre-2010 leak-free prior built only from the three pre-2010 BCGS Cu+ holes in the subarea (sparse but free of temporal leakage). Right: uniform prior (planner starts blind). Bars show percent of the 14 post-2010 Cu+ cells captured in the top-k% of each policy's trajectory. With the informative prior the static recommendation dominates at 86%; with the uniform prior the sparse-positive regime resists all policies; the leak-free prior sits in between.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_b2_retrospective_capture.png)

The reading. With the informative prior, sorting cells by the prior
alone (Static prior) captures 86% of the 14 post-2010 Cu+ cells in
the top 25%. POMCP gets to 79%, BayesianGreedy to 43%. The v2.0
Bayesian update via GP correlation does NOT improve on a near-perfect
prior; the particle-filter posterior introduces Monte Carlo noise
that the static surface doesn't have. With the leak-free pre-2010
prior the static recommendation still leads at 50%, but the gap to
POMCP (36%) and BayesianGreedy (29%) is much smaller. With the
uniform prior, the positives are too sparse (~0.6% of cells) for
any policy to learn from random observations within the trajectory
budget; the variance dominates the comparison.

Regional features carry most of the signal at BCGT scale. A planner
is only as good as the prior it's given, and at single-hypothesis
B.2 the prior IS most of the answer. The v2.0 machinery's natural
use case is uncertainty in prior STRUCTURE itself, which is the C.2
multi-hypothesis case (planned). The retrospective also surfaces a
methodology caveat: MINFILE is a current snapshot, not a pre-2010
one, so the informative-prior numbers include some temporal leakage
from post-2010 discoveries that became MINFILE entries. The
leak-free panel is the cleaner absolute comparison.

## Limits

The planner here is single-hypothesis: it carries one geological
story and updates per-cell probability estimates within that story.
The version that handles "what if the prior is wrong", with
competing geological hypotheses and a falsification check that
fires when none of them explain the data, is milestone C.2; that
work extends the toy demonstration shipped in the deeper notebook
to production scale.

The retrospective B.2 working area is one 50x50 cell subarea around
the KSM cluster. Other clusters in the BCGT and other deposit types
(epithermal Au, skarn, VMS) would each give a separate check; the
chapter ships one to demonstrate the machinery rather than to
characterize the planner's behavior across all BCGT terrains.

The property-scale counterpart of this idea, a Gaussian process
drill-site recommender on the 24 Bear Cub holes, sits in [Chapter 1](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html).
The Bear Cub recommender produces a workable next-hole pair even
though the spatial-only GP predicts pay-zone grade poorly in
absolute terms, because the posterior variance is a faithful map
of where the data is sparse even when the mean is unreliable.

## How the policies hold up under noisy binary sensors

The B.1 chart at the top uses a Gaussian-continuous sensor (a drill
returns a grade reading + noise). Real drill assays come back as
quantified values, but in a hand-mapping or first-look reconnaissance
the operator decision is often binary: above or below the cutoff.
Mern et al. 2024 model that case with a Bernoulli sensor with two
sensitivity parameters, alpha (the false-positive rate) and beta (the
false-negative rate). C.1 implements that sensor branch and sweeps
the (alpha, beta) plane on a 3x3 grid.

![Mean discovery rate per policy across 9 (alpha, beta) combinations on the same 30x30 synthetic subarea as the B.1 chart. Random sits at 0.06 across the whole plane and GreedyMean sits at 0.32: neither uses observations, so they don't respond to sensor quality. BayesianGreedy ranges 0.48-0.54 and POMCP ranges 0.43-0.57 depending on the sensor parameters; both policies degrade modestly as the sensor gets noisier, and POMCP holds a small edge over BayesianGreedy at most cells. The within-row variation includes 17-episode Monte Carlo noise; the relative ordering is the chapter point, not the precise cell values.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_c1_bernoulli_sweep.png)

The reading. Even with substantial sensor noise (10% false positive
plus 20% false negative, the worst cell), the v2.0 machinery extracts
most of the value from observations: BayesianGreedy and POMCP land
roughly 18 to 25 points above the static-prior baseline (0.32). The
sensitivity to sensor quality is modest: dropping the false-positive
rate from 10% to 2% buys at most a few discovery-rate points. The
single-hypothesis B.1 finding holds under the Bernoulli sensor: most
of the win is correlation-aware belief updating; multi-step lookahead
is a smaller increment on top.

## When the prior itself is wrong

The B.1, B.2, and C.1 charts assume the planner's prior is a fair
approximation of the truth, even when noisy. C.2 handles the harder
case: what if neither of the prior's competing geological hypotheses
is right? Mern et al. 2024 add a null hypothesis (h_0, a maximum-
entropy mixture with no spatial structure) alongside the paper
hypotheses and update a categorical posterior over the whole set
after every drill. When the null becomes the most-supported
hypothesis, the planner flags "the geological model doesn't explain
what we're seeing" rather than continuing to fit a wrong story.

![Categorical posterior over a 3-hypothesis set across 25 random drills under three ground-truth scenarios. Left: ground truth drawn from hypothesis A; the posterior locks onto h_A by drill 3. Center: ground truth from hypothesis B; h_B wins by drill 5-7. Right: ground truth from the null (no spatial structure); the falsification flag fires at drill 2 because both paper hypotheses immediately fail to explain observations the null predicts.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_c2_falsification_demo.png)

The reading. The falsification check fires fast when neither paper
hypothesis matches the data. The two paper-hypothesis scenarios also
converge cleanly to the correct hypothesis within 5-7 drills, which
demonstrates that the categorical update mechanism doesn't degrade
the planner's identification ability.

## Pre-computed alpha-vector policies under the multi-hypothesis prior

POMCP runs a fresh Monte Carlo tree search at each drill, which works
but pays the planning cost online and is sensitive to the rollout
budget. SARSOP (Kurniawati, Hsu, Lee 2008) solves the same POMDP
once offline and returns a set of alpha vectors that compile to a
microsecond-per-step online policy. Mern et al. 2024 argue this matters
at the multi-hypothesis scale: POMCP's value-function variance grows
with the number of hypotheses, while SARSOP's offline computation is
amortized.

My first attempt at a multi-hypothesis comparison was a 5 by 5 grid
with 4 corner-deposit hypotheses plus a null, sensor settings matched
to C.1, and a 9-drill budget. SARSOP, POMCP, and greedy-MAP all tied
at 74% discovery. The reason was the experiment design: each
hypothesis placed its deposit at a unique cell with no penalty for
guessing wrong, so greedy-MAP's "drill the highest-expected-deposit
cell" policy was already optimal. Multi-step lookahead had nothing to
add. SARSOP did show its other property: 1.4 milliseconds per
decision versus POMCP's 16, after a 0.4-second offline solve.

To actually expose the planning advantage I rebuilt the experiment as
a Tiger-style problem. Two hypotheses, A and B, each with a "claim"
cell that pays +50 when right and -30 when wrong, and two "marker"
cells that return an informative sensor reading but no reward. A
claim cell stands in for a drill target each competing geological
model would recommend: Hypothesis A says "the ore is here," and
claim A is the cell you would drill under that model. The
wrong-commitment penalty represents what happens in real exploration
when you burn a $1M hole on the wrong target while the actual
deposit sits undrilled. A marker cell stands in for a piece of
discriminating evidence the two models predict differently: a
magnetic anomaly, a geochemical halo, a structural feature, a
logging response. The two models disagree about where the deposit
is, and they also disagree about what you would see at certain other
cells. The claims are pure commit actions; the markers are pure
information. A uniform prior, a 5-drill budget, and the same
Bernoulli sensor noise as the earlier experiment. This is the case
where the wiring earns its keep: greedy under uniform belief picks
one of the claim cells immediately and pays the wrong-commitment
penalty on half the episodes, while a smart planner spends one drill
on the appropriate marker and commits only after the posterior
concentrates.

![Three-panel comparison on the Tiger-style problem. Left: mean discounted reward with one-standard-error bars. Center: how often each policy drilled the wrong claim cell. Right: discovery rate (right claim eventually drilled within the budget). 200 episodes, gamma=0.95, sensor (alpha, beta) = (0.10, 0.10), wrong-commitment penalty 30.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_sarsop_marker_advantage.png)

The reading. Greedy-MAP averages +1 reward, drills the wrong claim on
56% of episodes, and discovers the right claim on only 45% (it
commits early and gets stuck drilling the wrong cell for the rest of
the budget). POMCP and SARSOP both use the markers to disambiguate
first: POMCP averages +31 with a 28% wrong-commit rate, and SARSOP
averages +37 with a 2% wrong-commit rate. SARSOP comes out ahead of
POMCP here because the alpha-vector policy is computed against the
full belief space rather than an MCTS sample of it; on a small
problem with a high penalty for committing under uncertainty, that
extra precision matters. The gap closes on harder problems where the
offline solve becomes the bottleneck.

The widget below plays one episode at a time so the mechanism is
visible turn-by-turn. Each panel shows the same 4 cells (two claims,
two markers), each policy's belief over Hypothesis A vs B as a
colored bar, the cell currently being drilled, and the running
reward. Press Play, then step backward or forward by drill. Greedy
locks onto a claim cell and stays there because drilling a claim
returns no information about which hypothesis is true. SARSOP picks
the appropriate marker first, the belief swings, then it commits.
You will see SARSOP sometimes drill the same marker twice before
committing: a single positive reading at a marker still leaves a
10% chance the sensor false-positived, and a second positive reading
cuts that to about 1%. The solver decides the extra drill is worth
it because it reduces the cost of a wrong commit on the next step.
You will also see policies re-drill cells after they have already
found the deposit: the toy problem fixes the budget at 5 drills
without a "stop" action, so re-drilling a known-good cell is the
least-bad use of leftover budget.

{{< include _widget_v20_marker_animation.qmd >}}

## Scaling up to the BCGT grid

The Tiger problem proved the machinery works on a 4-cell toy. The
next question is whether the same approach scales onto the actual
BCGT subarea: a 30 by 30 grid (15 km by 15 km at 500 m per cell),
with the same GP kernel parameters the rest of the chapter uses
(Matern v=2.5, sigma=0.1, l=1500 m). Directly handing SARSOP a 900-
cell action space would not terminate in any reasonable time: the
alpha-vector count grows fast with state and action dimensionality,
and 900 actions are too many.

The fix is belief-conditioned action pruning. At each drill step the
planner computes the expected deposit probability per cell under its
current categorical belief over hypotheses, picks the top K = 20
un-drilled cells by that expectation, compresses those cells into a
small POMDP (K actions, N hypothesis states, binary observations),
and solves it with SARSOP. The candidate set changes every step as
the belief updates; the offline solve runs each step on the new
subproblem. On the 30 by 30 setup with 3 hypotheses, that subproblem
solves in 0.02 seconds. A full 9-drill episode costs under a second
of planning time.

To probe the scale-up I built two synthetic GP-correlated hypotheses
on the BCGT grid plus the null. H_NW places a Gaussian deposit
anomaly in the northwest quadrant; H_SE places one in the southeast
quadrant. Both share the same kernel parameters; only the prior mean
fields differ.

![D.1.A: the three priors on the BCGT subarea, plus the expected deposit probability under a uniform belief over the three hypotheses. Red markers show the top 20 candidate cells SARSOP would consider first under the uniform belief: they split between the two blob centers, because the planner has no reason yet to favor either hypothesis.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_d1_priors_setup.png)

The benchmark runs 30 episodes with truth drawn uniformly from
{H_NW, H_SE, H_null}, a 9-drill budget, gamma = 0.95, and the same
Bernoulli sensor noise as the earlier experiments. Both planners
(POMCP and SARSOP) operate on the same top-K=20 candidate set per
step, so the comparison isolates decision quality rather than
candidate-pool composition.

![D.1.C: BCGT-scale benchmark. Left: overall cumulative reward across the 30 episodes, one-standard-error bars. Center: reward broken down by ground truth (H_NW, H_SE, H_null). Right: posterior weight on the true hypothesis at the end of each episode.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_d1_benchmark.png)

The reading. Overall, SARSOP averages +189, POMCP +181, greedy +132,
and random +99. The 57-point gap between SARSOP and greedy lives
entirely in the middle panel's H_SE column. Under H_NW truth (10
episodes) greedy ties SARSOP at +361, because greedy's
uniform-prior-tiebreaking happens to send it to NW candidate cells,
which are exactly where the deposit lives. Under H_SE truth (7
episodes) greedy collapses to +61: it still commits to NW first,
spends 3-4 drills there getting negative readings without updating
the belief much (each negative is small evidence), and runs out of
budget before reaching the SE blob. SARSOP and POMCP belief-update
fast enough that one or two informative negatives are sufficient to
switch their next pick to the SE candidate set; they recover to
+307 and +330 respectively. Under H_null truth (13 episodes) every
policy loses the same 7-point floor: nine drills' worth of
discounted cost without ever finding a deposit, because no deposit
exists.

Per-step planning latency at this scale: SARSOP 9.7 ms, POMCP 18.5
ms. SARSOP runs about twice as fast as POMCP because the alpha-
vector policy compiles to a dot product against the belief, while
POMCP runs 200 simulations per step. Greedy is 0.2 ms.

One caveat to read this fairly: the synthetic priors I built have
well-separated blobs (no overlap between H_NW and H_SE deposit
cells), which is the easy case for any planner that can
disambiguate hypotheses. Real BCGS deposit-type-split priors will
overlap heavily, because an alteration halo can sit inside two
competing geological model domains at once. The next extension
swaps the synthetic priors for real ones derived from the dh2loop
deposit-type labels and re-runs the same comparison.

## Why v1.0 tied

The 2023 v1.0 paper used independent Bernoulli cells (no correlation
between neighbors) and a noiseless sensor. Under those conditions the
optimal sequential policy IS exactly one-step greedy: drilling reveals
truth, no observation updates any other cell, and there is nothing
for multi-step planning to add. An earlier version of this page showed
that result with POMCP and greedy tying. The synthetic chart at the
top of this page runs the 2024 v2.0 setup that the same authors
published a year later, adding correlation and a noisy sensor.

## Next steps

The Mern 2024 port is now feature-complete through C.2 and validated
at BCGT scale with synthetic priors. The one remaining piece is
swapping the synthetic NW-blob-vs-SE-blob hypotheses for real BCGS
deposit-type splits derived from the dh2loop labels covered in
Chapter 2. Real deposit-type priors will overlap heavily across
hypotheses (an alteration halo can sit inside two competing model
domains at once), which is the harder test the synthetic priors do
not pose. The deeper notebook carries the implementation roadmap.

## Deeper notebooks

- [BCGT POMDP decision planning](./notebooks/bcgt/decision_planning.html): full implementation, GP kernel choice, particle-filter mechanics, the four-policy benchmark code, and the milestone roadmap details.

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
